questions.question: Why does an LSTM use a cell state with mostly additive interactions?
options: Additive flow lets gradients propagate through long sequences without vanishing or exploding
questions.explanation: The cell-state highway keeps gradients stable across hundreds of steps, fixing the vanishing-gradient problem that cripples plain RNNs.

questions.question: Why does TextCNN use global max-pooling after the convolutional layer?
options: Max-pool gives a fixed-size, position-invariant representation by selecting the strongest activation per filter
questions.explanation: Global max-pool produces a fixed-size feature vector regardless of input length, with position invariance — 'not good' fires the same feature wherever it appears.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of a bidirectional RNN over a one-way RNN for sequence labeling?
options: Each token's representation sees both left and right context, which is essential for tagging
questions.explanation: Bidirectional networks concatenate forward + backward hidden states so every token's label can draw on full context in both directions.

questions.question: Why does max-pooling over an LSTM's outputs often beat using just the last hidden state for classification?
options: Max-pool is differentiable; last-state is not
Information at the end of a long sequence tends to dominate the last state, hiding earlier evidence
questions.explanation: Max-pool aggregates the strongest signal across all positions; the last hidden state can lose earlier evidence under recency pressure in long sequences.

questions.question: Which limitation of LSTM/RNN encoder-decoders motivated attention?
options: The decoder sees only a fixed-size encoder state, losing detail on long inputs; recurrence also serializes training
questions.explanation: Fixed-size context-vector summarization loses long-input detail, and serial recurrence prevents parallel training — both failures that attention and transformers removed.

questions.question: When does a TextCNN or BiLSTM still beat a transformer in 2026?
options: Edge / on-device, streaming token-by-token inputs, or tiny-data baselines
When latency requirements are extremely loose
questions.explanation: Small architectures win on edge inference (10-100x smaller than transformers), streaming inputs where full-sequence context is unavailable, and rapid CPU baselines.
